Title: Interdisciplinary Integration Of Knowledge Construction Based On The Arcs Motivational Model In Ai-Driven Gamified Learning Environments
Author: Youyou1; Chen Zihan1; Huang Mengfang11 School of Art, Northwestern University,Shaanxi,China
Abstract: Conference Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Education (ICAIE) Conference Start Date: 2025 May 14 Conference End Date: 2025 May 16 Conference Location: Suzhou, China The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) technology is fundamentally reshaping educational paradigms. AI-driven gamified learning environments demonstrate unique potential in facilitating interdisciplinary knowledge construction through two key mechanisms: (1) the strategic integration of game mechanics into pedagogical processes, and (2) the systematic application of AI capabilities to transcend traditional disciplinary boundaries. This study establishes a theoretical framework synthesizing gamification principles with interdisciplinary education theory, with particular emphasis on the ARCS (Attention, Relevance, Confidence, Satisfaction) motivation model and intelligent educational agents. Our proposed framework employs AI algorithms to generate adaptive learning scenarios and multimodal interaction systems designed to achieve dual objectives: cognitive engagement through attention-capturing mechanics and pedagogical alignment through personalized content delivery. The research further develops a three-phase implementation strategy for interdisciplinary knowledge integration within gamified contexts: (1) contextual bridging across disciplines, (2) schema reconstruction through problem-based challenges, and (3) meta-cognitive reinforcement via intelligent feedback systems. Empirical evidence from our experimental implementation reveals statistically significant improvements in both learner engagement metrics (p<0.01, Cohen's d=1.2) and knowledge transfer scores (p<0.05, η 2 =0.32), demonstrating the framework's efficacy in operationalizing motivation theory while addressing contemporary educational challenges. These findings not only advance theoretical understanding of technology-enhanced learning but also provide actionable insights for designing next-generation educational systems that effectively bridge disciplinary divides.

Title: Resume Named Entity Recognition Based On Chatglm-9B Fine-Tuning
Author: Liu, Xin1; Qiu, Quantong1; Jiang, Ruiling1; Lv, Jian11 Jiangxi University of Finance and Economics,Nanchang,China
Abstract: Conference Title: 2024 7th International Conference on Mechatronics and Computer Technology Engineering (MCTE) Conference Start Date: 2024 Aug. 23 Conference End Date: 2024 Aug. 25 Conference Location: Guangzhou, China Named Entity Recognition (NER) technology is a key technique for resume information extraction, and there are current issues with resume NER algorithms such as poor knowledge transfer ability, large model parameter volume, and long model training time. This paper proposes a new method based on Rora instruction fine-tuning of large language models combined with situation simulation. By introducing low-rank matrices to simulate the effect of full-parameter fine-tuning, and transforming the Resume dataset into a knowledge question-and-answer format that integrates situation simulation, the fine-tuned large language model is more suitable for the application scenarios of resume entity recognition. Experiments show that the F1 score on the Resume dataset that integrates situation simulation reached 95.14%. Compared to several models that perform well in the task of resume named entity recognition, the improvements were 5.72%, 4.38%, and 1.62% respectively, thereby validating that the method can effectively leverage the advantages of NLG (Natural Language Generation) language models in text generation to accomplish the task of named entity recognition.

Title: An Ai Chatbot For Enhanced Learning Through Speech, Summarization, And Interactive Quizzing
Author: Suresh Dara1; Thiran Bhavani Naidu2; Valiveti, Varshini2; Gaddala, Nidhi Joyel2; Pandi, Geethika21 VIT-AP University,School of Computer Science and Engineering,Amaravati,India,5222412 VIT-AP University,Computer Science and Engineering,Amaravati,India,522241
Abstract: Conference Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Recent Advances in Information Technology (RAIT) Conference Start Date: 2025 March 6 Conference End Date: 2025 March 8 Conference Location: Dhanbad, India The “An AI Chatbot for Enhanced Learning Through Speech, Summarization, and Interactive Quizzing” also known as AI Teacher, introduces a cutting-edge educational platform that enhances learning through the integration of advanced AI tools. This innovative solution combines text summarization, voice synthesis, and automated quiz generation to create a dynamic and engaging learning environment for students and educators alike. The first module utilizes the BART (Bidirectional and Auto-Regressive Transformers) model to distill complex or lengthy texts into clear, concise summaries. These summaries are further converted into speech, providing an auditory learning alternative that supports varied learning preferences and promotes accessibility. The second module focuses on deepening understanding through active engagement. It uses the Google Gemini API to transform the generated summaries into multiplechoice questions (MCQs). These quizzes facilitate knowledge reinforcement and self-assessment, allowing users to evaluate their grasp of the material. This automated process reduces the workload for educators while encouraging learners to interact more actively with the content. This article provides a unified solution that simplifies information processing, enhances comprehension, and supports personalized learning paths. By leveraging AI-driven tools, the platform fosters inclusive education, making learning more accessible and interactive. This paper a forward-thinking approach to educational technology, offering a robust framework for improved knowledge retention and engagement. It equips both educators and learners with the tools to achieve more effective, efficient, and enjoyable educational outcomes.

Title: Artificial Intelligence Ecosystem For Automating Self-Directed Teaching
Author: Tejas Satish Gotavade
Abstract: This research introduces an innovative artificial intelligence-driven educational concept designed to optimize self-directed learning through personalized course delivery and automated teaching assistance. The system leverages fine-tuned AI models to create an adaptive learning environment that encompasses customized roadmaps, automated presentation generation, and three-dimensional modeling for complex concept visualization. By integrating real-time virtual assistance for doubt resolution, the platform addresses the immediate educational needs of learners while promoting autonomous learning practices. This study explores the psychological advantages of self-directed learning and demonstrates how AI automation can enhance educational outcomes through personalized content delivery and interactive support mechanisms. The research contributes to the growing field of educational technology by presenting a comprehensive framework that combines automated content generation, visual learning aids, and intelligent tutoring to create an efficient, scalable solution for modern educational needs. Preliminary findings suggest that this approach not only accommodates diverse learning styles but also strengthens student engagement and knowledge retention through its emphasis on self-paced, independent learning methodologies.

Title: Need For Generational Shift In Teaching Methods And Its Application In Orthopedics: Why Medical Education Should Keep Up With Generationz
Author: Priyanshu1; Barik, Sitanshu1; Dubepuria, Amol1; Mohabey, Ankush1; Madegowda, Arkesh2; Das, Lakshmana S31 Orthopedics, AIIMS, Nagpur, India2 Orthopedics, AIIMS, Raipur, India3 Orthopedics, AIIMS, Bathinda, India
Abstract: The rapid integration of Generation Z (GenZ) into medical education has highlighted the need for a transformative shift in teaching methodologies, particularly in specialized fields like orthopedics. As digital natives, GenZ learners-born between 1995 and 2010-exhibit distinct preferences for interactive, technology-driven, and self-directed learning, rendering traditional lecture-based models increasingly obsolete. This scoping review explores the pedagogical implications of GenZ's learning traits and advocates for a generational shift in orthopedic education to align with their cognitive styles and career aspirations. GenZ students thrive in environments that use digital tools such as virtual reality, augmented reality, and gamified platforms, which provide immersive, risk-free surgical simulations and improve procedural mastery. Microlearning techniques, including bite-sized videos and infographics support their shorter attention spans and improve retention of complex topics like fracture classifications and biomechanics. Collaborative learning strategies, such as team-based problem-solving and peer-teaching models, resonate with their preference for social interaction and teamwork. Additionally, adaptive learning systems powered by artificial intelligence offer personalized study plans, addressing individual strengths and weaknesses. The review demonstrates the importance of early clinical exposure and simulation-based training to bridge theory and practice, encouraging pragmatic, career-ready professionals. Educators, particularly millennials, must embrace digital fluency and mentorship to create learner-centered environments. By integrating these innovations-flipped classrooms, gamification, and on-demand resources-orthopedic education can lead to engagement, knowledge retention, and clinical preparedness. Ultimately, this generational shift is imperative to equip future orthopedic surgeons with the skills demanded by modern healthcare. Embracing technology, interactivity, and personalized learning will redefine training approaches, ensuring optimal educational outcomes and patient care for the next generation of medical professionals.The rapid integration of Generation Z (GenZ) into medical education has highlighted the need for a transformative shift in teaching methodologies, particularly in specialized fields like orthopedics. Title: Ai Powered Integrated Code Repository Analyzer For Efficient Developer Workflow
Author: Akalanka, Isuru1; De Silva, Sandeepa1; Ganeshalingam, Mayuran1; Abeykoon, Akash1; Wijendra, Dinuka1; Krishara, Jenny11 Sri Lanka Institute of Information Technology,Faculty of Computing,Malabe,Sri Lanka
Abstract: Conference Title: 2025 International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE) Conference Start Date: 2025 April 3 Conference End Date: 2025 April 3 Conference Location: Colombo, Sri Lanka Transitioning between new and legacy codebases in diverse project environments poses significant challenges for developers, especially with traditional Knowledge Transfer (KT) methods, which are often resource intensive and prone to obsolescence. These limitations hinder the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), particularly in fast-paced industrial settings. This research introduces an AI-driven automation solution that leverages large language models (LLMs) and advanced artificial intelligence technologies to address critical gaps in technical knowledge transfer, with a focus on modern software frameworks. The proposed system reduces development costs, improves team performance, and accelerates adaptation to complex codebases. Key features include a documentation generation tool that cuts manual effort by up to 90%, with an average generation time of 6.8 minutes. Additionally, a virtual knowledge transfer assistant enhances onboarding efficiency, potentially reducing senior developer involvement by 50-60%. The system also includes an automated diagram generator that achieves 97% validation accuracy and a code smell detection tool with 71% accuracy, resulting in better code quality assessments. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of AI-driven automation in improving developer productivity, streamlining onboarding processes, and optimizing software development workflows.

Title: Ai-Powered Transcript Summarization And Question Answering For Youtube And Wikipedia
Author: Sharma, Kanishka1; Srivastava, Sarthak1; Pilania, Harsh1; Maurya, Pranjal Kumar1; Ayushi Prakash21 AKGEC Engineering College,CSE Department,Ghaziabad,India2 AKGEC Engineering College,Computer Science and Engineering,Ghaziabad,India
Abstract: Conference Title: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence, Communication Technology and Networking (CICTN) Conference Start Date: 2025, Feb. 6 Conference End Date: 2025, Feb. 7 Conference Location: Ghaziabad, India With the rapid growth of online content, there is an increasing demand for efficient tools that facilitate content understanding and knowledge retention. This research focuses on the development of an AI-powered system designed to automate summarization, question answering, and quiz generation from YouTube transcripts and Wikipedia articles. The system employs advanced natural language processing (NLP) techniques to process and distill complex information into clear and concise summaries. The process begins with the extraction of text through the YouTube Transcript API or web scraping. The Pegasus-XSum model is then used for summarizing the content, ensuring that key information is retained while minimizing text length. For question answering, the Llama model is employed to generate contextually relevant answers based on the provided content. Additionally, the system generates quizzes by extracting entities from the text to enhance interactive learning. This technology aims to improve content comprehension, making it especially valuable for educational settings and knowledge management. By integrating summarization, question answering, and quiz generation into a single platform, the system supports efficient learning and better retention of critical information. This research demonstrates the potential of NLP in addressing the challenges of information overload and facilitating effective knowledge acquisition.

Title: Transformative Pedagogies: Educational Innovations In Cybersecurity
Author: Zenzo Polite Ncube1; Mpofu, Nontokozo2; Nxumalo, Madoda A11 University of Eswatini,Department of Computer Science,Kwaluseni,Eswatini2 Sol Plaatje University,Department of Computer Science,Kimberley,South Africa
Abstract: Conference Title: 2024 4th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC) Conference Start Date: 2024, Nov. 27 Conference End Date: 2024, Nov. 29 Conference Location: Vanderbijlpark, South Africa As the digital landscape undergoes continual transformation, the necessity for adept cybersecurity professionals has reached unprecedented levels. This paper investigates the convergence of education and cybersecurity. It offers a thorough analysis of avant-garde pedagogical approaches geared towards equipping the forthcoming generation of cyber defenders. Our study delves into cutting-edge educational strategies that transcend conventional cybersecurity curricula. It embraces dynamic methodologies such as hands-on learning, immersive simulations, and collaborative problem-solving. Moreover, we explore the seamless integration of emerging technologies that include virtual labs, gamification, and interactive scenarios in order to foster the cultivation of practical skills. Furthermore, this study evaluates the tangible impacts of educational innovations on knowledge retention, skills development, and adaptability to the ever-evolving realm of cyber threats. Through the examination of case studies and empirical evidence gathered, we spotlight the successful implementations of these innovations across diverse educational landscapes, spanning from academic institutions to professional training programs. In addition to the above, our research dissects the pivotal roles played by mentorship, industry partnerships, and real-world applications in nurturing a holistic educational environment. By unravelling the symbiotic relationship between academia and industry, we ensure that educational advancements remain finely attuned to the dynamic demands of the cybersecurity landscape.

Title: Development Of Guidance Documents In The Eurad And Predi S Projects
Author: Ormai, Peter; Nős, Bálint; Faltejsek, Jiří; Mikšová, Jitka; Železnik, Nadja; Mele, Irena; Fuzik, Kateryna; Carbol, Paul; Banford, Anthony; Holt, Erika
Abstract: Particular emphasis is dedicated to Knowledge Management activities within the EURAD (European Joint Programme on Radioactive Waste Management) and PREDIS (Pre-disposal management of radioactive waste) projects to ensure the capture of existing knowledge, transfer of knowledge between Members States and management of the knowledge for future generations. The EURAD project has three work packages dedicated to knowledge management. One of them, the EURAD Guidance work package (WP12) is developing a comprehensive suite of specific guidance documents that can be used by Members States with radioactive waste management (RWM) programmes that are at an early stage of development but can be beneficial also to more advanced programmes. The PREDIS project does not have a specifically allocated work package for guidance development. Rather, such activities are integrated within deliverables produced as part of the Strategic Implementation and State of Knowledge actions of the Roadmap contributions on predisposal waste management. The EURAD guidance work is based on the existing PLANDIS guide on RD&D planning, developed by the Implementing Geological Disposal of Radioactive Waste Technology Platform (IGD-TP). The guidance documents complement other documents prepared in parallel knowledge management activities inside EURAD project: the State of Knowledge documents. The differentiation is that guidance documents explain in more detail how the process can be established and performed, including illustrative examples. The guides are self-standing documents and integrated with the EURAD Roadmap. The target end users of the guidance are primarily programme owners and managers (i.e., governments/administrations, Waste Management Organisations, Research Entities and Technical Support Organisations) responsible for planning and implementing the RWM programme and the supporting RD&D activities at a national level, even though they might also be of use and interest to other interested stakeholders, such as representatives of civil society. To produce a first list of prioritised topics for guidance documents with the aim to select a topic for a pilot guide, the Guidance WP has developed a screening process that includes review by experts and end users. Based on the priority list, the first pilot guide was developed with the title “Cost Assessment and Financing Schemes of Radioactive Waste Management Programmes”. Experience gained during the selection of topics for the pilot guide and during its production are being incorporated into the procedure for identification of new topics for which guides will be developed. First, the degree of coverage of the EURAD Roadmap themes by suitable guide documents will be analysed by the WP 12 team. The analysis will be combined with feedback from experts verifying the needs for missing guides. Finally, the potential end user community representatives will be given the opportunity to comment on the prioritisation of selected guidance documents and make additional suggestions. The potential end users stay involved also during the production of the guides. This procedure aims to optimise the scarce expert resources in relation to the identified needs of guidance documents. This article explains the approach for selecting topics for guidance documents and the results obtained both in EURAD and PREDIS.

Title: Knowledge Transfer Dynamics In Family Businesses Through Digitalisation: A Systematic Literature Review
Author: Talarico, Alessia1; Lorena, Antonio; Zoccali, Ludovica; Ventura, Marzia1 University Magna Græcia of Catanzaro, Catanzaro, Italy
Abstract: In the current unpredictable and constantly evolving scenario, knowledge is a strategic resource for businesses (Argote L., Ingram P., 2000), particularly in the case of family businesses (Zapata - Cantu L. et al., 2023). In this context knowledge transfer is most important and technology and innovation can concretely support the transfer of information, both inside and outside organizations; but family businesses are not very technology-oriented (Bouncken В. & Schmitt F., 2022). So, the aim of this study is to understand how digitalization could impact on knowledge transfer dynamics in family businesses and in particular on codifying explicit knowledge, on transfer of tacit knowledge and on generational succession in family businesses.

Title: The Use Of Ai For Education In Third Age: The Role Of Eu Project
Author: Spulber, Diana1; Amoretti, Guido1; Siri, Anna21 University of Genoa, Italy2 Pegaso Telematic University, Naples, Italy
Abstract: Society today is struggling with various challenging phenomena. This manuscript aims to analyse two phenomena, ageing and digitalisation, in the context of education. Both phenomena are an important area of research: the complexity of an ageing population and the intersection of technology and education. In this context, artificial intelligence (AI) offers the opportunity to improve the educational experiences of older adults. AI-driven tools and educational initiatives can be tailored to the different learning preferences and cognitive abilities of this population, promoting engagement and knowledge retention. The adaptability of AI systems enables personalised learning pathways that address individual challenges and promote lifelong learning and cognitive health in older learners. This research will highlight the critical role of AI in reshaping educational paradigms and its potential to facilitate knowledge acquisition and empower an often-marginalised population. Finally, focusing on the role of international projects will allow us to understand the trends in EU research and funding. Understanding the impact of AI on education in the third age and EU funding invites a broader discussion on inclusivity and innovation in the educational landscape.

Title: Tapping Into Retirees To Bolster Knowledge Transfer
Author: Ritter, Beth M, CCP1; Clark, Robert L, PhD11 North Carolina State University
Abstract: Preservation of knowledge by an organization is critical for ongoing success. The knowledge transfer between retirees and their entity's remaining employees offers a unique opportunity to retain knowledge. Employers that engage with employees prior to their retirement can allow for a longer and more productive knowledge transfer process. The aim of our research has been to learn how to make knowledge exchanges successful by considering methods. Managers should consider new efforts for knowledge-continuity management. Multiple surveys were designed to determine the extent of knowledge-loss risk associated with retirees, the prevalence of informal and formal knowledge-transfer practices and the effectiveness of knowledge transfer for knowledge continuity. Results showed that organizations perceive significant knowledge-loss risk with the impending wave of retirements from an aging workforce and that many are not proactively approaching this opportunity. Organizations are leveraging only a few knowledge-transfer approaches and not leveraging technology for tacit knowledge transfers to occur. The data indicate that there is considerable room for improvement in knowledge-transfer practices - and our research offers practical implications for effective knowledge-transfer activities.

Title: An Innovative Approach To A More Sustainable And Reliable Energy Society Demonstrated By Renewable Energy Sources For Smart Sustainable Health Centers, University Education And Other Public Buildings
Author: Dumnic, Boris11 University of Novi Sad, Faculty of Technical Sciences, Electronics and Telecommunications Engineering,Department for Power,Novi Sad,Serbia
Abstract: Conference Title: 2022 International Conference on Smart Systems and Technologies (SST) Conference Start Date: 2022, Oct. 19 Conference End Date: 2022, Oct. 21 Conference Location: Osijek, Croatia By reviewing current criteria of a modern society it is not hard to demonstrate that most of the qualifiers and quantifiers revolve around energy related trivia. It is hard to imagine a future for the humanity where significant efforts were not made to tackle the two most important questions: sustainability of energy supply and reliability of energy supply (with the opportune issue being energy independence). These issues will be highly influenced by the current state of the related technologies that has been developing swiftly. Predominantly, this relates to the energy generation technologies, however inherent benefits can be reaped from mutual co-benefits of other contemporary technologies such as energy storage, electrified transportation and others. The key to successful harnessing of the available potential lies within the inclusive and innovative approach to utilization of renewable energy sources in high energy demand public and private sectors, aggregated by comprehensive energy management solution that additionally assumes stationary energy storage and electrified transportation devices as intermittent storage. The innovative approach in creating a smart and sustainable energy society of the future will be demonstrated through key results of the Renewable Energy Sources for smart sustainable health Centers, University Education and other public buildings (RESCUE) project, with the most important component being transfer-ability and expansion of the results. Assuming the initial impulse from the regionally established knowledge transfer basis, the foundation for the climate-smart, emission-neutral sustainable energy society of the future has been laid. The key findings of the RESCUE projects can easily be applied to other public buildings, but in the wake of another energy crisis the benefits can also be acquired by private enterprises and SME's looking to downsize daily operational costs. Renewable energy generation and other associated technologies (storage, EV's etc.) present everyone with the opportunity for the paradigm shift (a change in perspective) and the development of novel climate friendly business models that can generate revenue in the same manner (if not better) as classical (fossil based) emission heavy solutions.

Abstract: Conference Title: 2022 3rd International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning (PRML) Conference Start Date: 2022, July 22 Conference End Date: 2022, July 24 Conference Location: Chengdu, China With the coming of intelligent era, the wide using of new generation information technology, such as Internet on Things, AI, Big data and Cloud computing, digital economic development is more and more prosperous for every economic entity, specially, for some emerging economic entities, such as RCEP nations. However, the problem of objective evaluation of ability of digital economic development of different nations and dependence of digital economic development among each nation are very hard but necessary under not leaking digital economic data of all parts. For solving this problem, an improvement Meta Learning approach was proposed creatively which constructed temporal dimension factor and spatial dimension factor based on spatial-temporal information matrix method. It could make knowledge transfer from source network to target network according to the orthogonal rule between temporal dimension factor and spatial dimension factor. 3 different type and smart test ways were designed in order to validate the performance of the approach of this paper comprehensively. Digital economic development data of RECP nations were collected and employed for testing. The amount and accuracy of knowledge transfer from different source network to various target network were validated one by one. The results not only validate the effectiveness of improving Meta Learning approach of this article but also reveal that the diversity of digital economy development among RECP nations but impacting tightly each other. Moreover, there also have relative contribution on opening knowledge transfer from source network to target network under privacy data not be leaked. This content would be researched at more complex industrial data forward.

Title: Preface
Author: Empty
Abstract: The International Conference of Sustainable Blue Economy (ICSBE) 2022 was part of a series of conferences started in 2020 that stress more in small islands issue, this time with the topic of “Promoting Sustainable Blue Economy by Protection – Production Strategy”. This kind of conference is vital in increasing the awareness of researcher and decision makers to answer the dependency of economy and ecosystem, including climate change. This conference allows us to discuss methods in creating a sustainable blue economy that harnesses the potential of our oceans and seas to improve the lives of all, and leverages the most recent innovations, scientific advances, and best practices to build prosperity while conserving our waters for future generations. Faculty of Fisheries and Marine Science of Pattimura University has successfully organized ICSBE 2022 conducted online on 27 October 2022 in Ambon Indonesia; consider that the offline conference will need to have more financial support. Furthermore, we are grateful particularly to our Rector of Pattimura University, Prof. Dr. M. J. Saptenno, our Dean, Dr. Yoisye Lopulalan, the staff members of Fisheries and Marine Science Faculty for their contribution and support to make this conference happened. The objective of this conference was to provide mean to have lectures, researches, government and other stakeholders to present and publish their work. As many as 152 participants from different institution in Indonesia and few from abroad attended the event virtually through a zoom scheme. The invited speakers came from different parts of the world, two from Indonesia, one each from United States, Japan, United Kingdom and Australia. The keynote speaker, Leonardo A. A. Teguh Sambodo, Ph.D., is the Director of Industry, Tourism, and Creative Economy, Ministry of National Development Planning/Development Planning Agency of Indonesia. He was given 25 minutes for his presentation with the title Indonesia’s Blue Economy Development Framework and 20 minutes for discussion. The rundown meeting continued into two plenary sessions for the invited speakers includes discussion (Q&A) for 95 and 80 minutes respectively. There were 30 papers presented in five parallel sessions, each presenter was having in average 10 minutes for presentation and 5 minutes for discussion. There was seven main topics in this meeting; namely fisheries and aquaculture, fishing technology, marine biodiversity and conservation, aquatic resource management, marine pollution and ocean health, fisheries socio economics, fisheries processing technology and ecotourism. We would like to thank all the participants for sharing their knowledge and experience, which we believe, will benefit as a catalyst for strengthening international cooperation on knowledge transfer. We also thank those who participate and help with the success of this conference. We look forward to meeting you at the next seminar. List of Organizing Committee are available in this pdf.

Title: Pictoquizezz: Engaging Cognitive Learning
Author: Gladiss Merlin N R1; Tamizh, Malar S, G1; Prathiksha, K1; Nivedha, P1; Pavithra, K11 R.M.K Engineering College,Department of ADS,Tiruvallur,Tamil Nadu
Abstract: Conference Title: 2023 International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE) Conference Start Date: 2023, Nov. 1 Conference End Date: 2023, Nov. 2 Conference Location: Chennai, India In the era of virtual interactions, finding innovative ways to engage participants in activities such as quizzes can be exciting. One captivating approach is hosting a virtual quiz without relying on keyboards and a mouse but solely utilizing webcams, active internet, and hand gestures. The virtual quiz utilizes computer vision algorithms to detect and track hand gestures through participants' webcams. By mapping specific gestures to different quiz actions, participants can select answers and interact with the quiz interface without the need for traditional input devices. Participants are presented with a series of questions displayed on the screen, and they can indicate their chosen answer by making specific hand gestures. Monitor the participants' webcam feeds attentively to identify their hand gestures. Provide immediate feedback on the accuracy of their responses, indicating whether they are correct or incorrect. This feedback mechanism ensures that the quiz serves as an educational tool, promoting a continuous learning experience. The use of AI in virtual quizzes can enhance the experience by providing personalized learning time-saving quiz generation, instant feedback, and adaptable difficulty. By leveraging webcam technology and hand gestures, this virtual quiz offers a novel and immersive approach to quiz participation. The virtual quiz employs computer vision algorithms to identify and monitor hand movements using participants' webcams. This innovative concept aims to create a fun and engaging experience for participants, encouraging active participation and knowledge retention through gesture-based interactions.
Keywords: Cognition; Webcam; Computer vision

Title: Building Of Computationally Effective Deep Learning Models Using Attention-Guided Knowledge Distillation
Author: Ashwath, V A1; Anish Sai Ayyagari1; Deebakkarthi, C R1; Arun, R Arumuga11 Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham,Amrita School of Computing,Department of Computer Science and En gineering,Coimbatore,India
Abstract: Conference Title: 2023 12th International Conference on Advanced Computing (ICoAC) Conference Start Date: 2023, Aug. 17 Conference End Date: 2023, Aug. 19 Conference Location: Chennai, India CNNs and Edge computing have proliferated in the last decade. While these technologies are stupendous in their own right, new horizons can be opened up if they were to be combined. Deep and complex CNNs, which are ones that produce groundbreaking accuracies, are not feasible for deployment in a resource constraint environment. The vanguard in model compression is an innovative technique known as Knowledge Distillation. This paper proposes a way to perform knowledge distillation by means of Attention Maps. The generation of attention maps from features is achieved through the use of ATCam. Not only does this method effectively distills knowledge it also nullifies another flaw of CNNs-black-boxiness. This may lead to leapfrogs in Mobile Machine Aided Medical Diagnosis by deploying interpretable complex CNN in small devices. Three ways to combine the feature maps were experimented with in this research work, which are Feature Map Accumulation(FMA), Maximum Intensity Projection(MIPs) and Saliency Maps. The performance of the proposed approaches are tested on PADUFES-20 dataset and all the three approaches provided significant boost in the performance over the traditional transfer learning methods. The best of these methods - saliency maps - gains a accuracy boost of 9.56% over the generic methods of knowledge transfer. Moreover, to one’s surprise, the resultant model not only matches the teacher model in accuracy, it exceeds it by 1.52% while being only 43.2% of original teacher model’s size.
Keywords: Deep learning; Maximum intensity projection; Knowledge distillation
Publication title: The Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers, Inc. (IEEE) Conference Proceedings; Piscataway

Title: Managing Smart Systems For The Net Zero Agenda – How Can Digital Twin Technologies And Smart Products Deliver Customer Value?
Author: Markey, Charlotte Lucinda1; Ahmed-Kristensen, Saeema11 University of Exeter
Abstract: Using the case study of a smart green blue roof pilot project in Greater Manchester, the authors have coded and analysed a series of semi structured interviews. We present our findings which are specifically focused on how 'smart' was understood by the multiple stakeholders involved in the project and how product complexity is managed when digital technologies are integrated into nature based solutions that are becoming more popularised in the construction sector. This integration of digital twin and sensor technologies with physical drainage products to create the next generation of smart green blue infrastructure, presents numerous challenges for organisations in the construction sector. We conclude that the need for smart systems of systems in this sector necessitates organisational change and new methods of knowledge transfer across organisations who work together to deliver the holistic physical and digital services to the client.
Keywords: Digital Twin; Smart products; Digital twin; Smart system; Construction; Smart Systems and Products; Sustainability; Collaborative design; Complexity
Publication title: Proceedings of the Design Society; Cambridge

Title: Samsax—An Innovative Living Lab For The Advancement Of A Circular Economy Through Additive Manufacturing Technologies
Author: Berkemeier, Adelina1    ; Kühnel, Lisa2; Dürigen, Dominik3; Hoffmann, Holger1    ; Zeidler, Henning2    ; Bullinger, Angelika C1; Wagenführ, André31 Ergonomics and Innovation Management, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Chemnitz University of Technology, 09125 Chemnitz, Germany; holger.hoffmann@mb.tu-chemnitz.de (H.H.); bullinger-hoffmann@mb.tu-chemnitz.de (A.C.B.)2 Additive Manufacturing, Institute for Machine Elements, Engineering Design and Manufacturing, TU Bergakademie Freiberg, 09599 Freiberg, Germany; lisa.kuehnel@imkf.tu-freiberg.de (L.K.); henning.zeidler@imkf.tu-freiberg.de (H.Z.)3 Wood Technology and Fibre Materials Technology, Dresden University of Technology, 01309 Dresden, Germany; dominik.duerigen@tu-dresden.de (D.D.); andre.wagenfuehr@tu-dresden.de (A.W.)
Abstract: The sustainable development of products is of great interest to both industry and consumers due to various factors, such as anthropogenic climate change and the scarcity of resources and materials. In response to this, the simul + Living Lab Sustainable Additive Manufacturing in Saxony (SAMSax) has been established as a physical experimental space aimed at improving the sustainability of products. This includes selecting resource-efficient manufacturing processes, using renewable materials, reducing energy consumption during use, and designing for recyclability. The innovative approach of the lab also integrates an open innovation process, involving present and potential stakeholders. Collaborating closely with stakeholders from industry, academia, and government fosters idea generation, provides solution approaches, and enhances acceptance and practical implementation. Methodologically, SAMSax focuses on upcycling organic and inorganic residues as well as by-products from industry and agriculture, reintegrating them as innovative components in industrial production using additive manufacturing (“3D printing”). The Living Lab provides a space for networking and active knowledge transfer through digital technologies, analyses, and collaborative developments, enabling the testing and evaluation of innovations in a real-world environment. Several potential waste materials suitable for additive manufacturing and new products have already been identified. In addition to industrial residues, materials, such as paper and wood dust; industrial by-products, such as sand; and agricultural residues, like harvest residues, are being analyzed, processed, and tested using additive manufacturing in the laboratory. In this way, SAMSax can contribute to an integrated and consistent circular economy. The research aims to demonstrate that the SAMSax Living Lab is a crucial driver of innovation in the field of additive manufacturing. Furthermore, this study contributes by presenting the Living Lab as an application-oriented research environment, focusing on innovative implementation in small- and medium-sized enterprises.
Keywords: Living Lab; 3D printing; Innovation; Living Lab; Circular economy; Manufacturing; Sustainability; additive manufacturing (AM); sustainability; circular economy; innovation process; digitalization

Title: Listening At Depth
Author: Carrier, Marie-Charlotte
Abstract: Answer to the Call is an installation created by artist Taloi Havini as part of her exhibition The Soul Expanding Ocean #1. Commissioned by the TBA21 Academy, Havini joined a scientific expedition aboard the research vessel Falkor to map the Great Barrier Reef using sonar technology. The installation, consisting of a twenty-two-channel sonic landscape, explores the symbiotic relationship between sound and its surroundings. Havini draws on traditional knowledge from her birthplace, Buka Island, to create a dialogue between different ways of knowing. The installation features hydrophone recordings, traditional marine chants, words spoken in Havini's first language, and an original score by composer Ben Hakalitz. The work reflects on the intergenerational loss of knowledge about the ocean and highlights the importance of deep listening. The installation immerses visitors in a sonic environment that evokes the interconnectedness of ocean and life systems.
Keywords: Soundscape; Medical ultrasound; Sonar; Listening; Depth

Title: An Adversarial Network Reinforcement Method For Power Data Time Series Generation Based On Knowledge Transfer And Rayleigh Differential Privacy
Author: Zhai, Feng1; Li, Baofeng1; Liang, Xiaobing1; Yu, Qin1; Ji, Wen21 China Electric Power Research Institute , State Grid, Beijing,100195, China2 Information and Communication Branch of State Grid Fujian Electric Power Co. , Ltd, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350013, China
Abstract: In order to solve the problem of sensitive information leakage of power data in machine learning and artificial intelligence processing, this paper proposes a method of civic-text data reinforcement based on time series generation adversarial network, which combines knowledge transfer and Rényi differential privacy protection framework to balance privacy and availability, while improving the accuracy of the model. First, the generative adversarial network generates training data that is similar to the real data but secure through adversarial learning, ensuring that even if the data is stolen, the original information cannot be inferred. Secondly, through the integration of recurrent neural network training and autoencoder, the dynamic and static characteristics of the power load data can be effectively captured, and the high dimension of the learning space can be reduced. In addition, knowledge distillation technology is used to guide the generator of training student models through a powerful teacher network of knowledge transfer, improving the diversity and quality of the generated data. Finally, from the four aspects of privacy, data similarity and diversity, and practicability, the practical application value and potential of the framework for electricity consumption data are comprehensively evaluated.

Title: E-Mentoring: A Mathematical Cohort For The Gen Z Higher Education Learners
Author: Empty
Abstract: Mentoring, a mechanism for knowledge and skill transfer is not new to people across the globe. In Indian context, the traditional Gurukula system is the best example of mentoring where the Sishya (mentee / protégé) who is usually a less experienced youth would serve the Guru (mentor), who is a teacher and an elderly person with rich experience for good knowledge transfer. The concept of relation in mathematics could be applied in e-mentoring. Though traditional face to face mentoring has advantages like ability to read the body language signals and facial expressions along with establishing a good rapport between mentor and mentee, E mentoring is proving its effectiveness in terms of solving issues in critical crisis period. From 594 students from AICTE approved institutions in Tamil Nadu, the response was recorded. Findings of the current research study makes it clear that the digital natives or the students belonging to Generation Z are more conversant with the latest technological developments and more curious about using them which makes them to feel convenient with e-mentoring in the place of traditional face-to-face mentoring. It may also open a door of opportunity to different sectors of students like students from different disciplines, students with special needs, destitute students and students from low socio economic background who belong to Generation Z.

Title: Building A Digital Altar For The Justice-Centered Torch-Bearers: The Role Of Digital Networks In Supporting Contemporary Racial Justice Activists Through Contemplative Practices
Author: Bradford, Renée L.
Abstract: This mixed-methods study aimed to identify factors critical in building a digital support network employing contemplative practices to support activists engaging in racial justice work in the United States. The research question that guided this study was: What factors do racial justice activists regard as critical in building a digital support network employing contemplative practices? The study?s importance lies in addressing the evolving landscape of digital activism while honoring the spiritual and relational foundations that have historically sustained social justice movements, particularly as contemporary activists navigate new technological tools while seeking to maintain authentic connection and contemplative grounding. Key findings revealed that activists prioritize relational elements over technological infrastructure, with clear communication channels ranking as the most critical factor, followed by accessibility and inclusivity, trust, and mutual support. The study identified nine major themes across focus groups, which emphasized that relational trust serves as the engine of sustainable activism, digital tools must serve the work rather than becoming barriers, and contemplative practices cultivate integrity for sustained justice work. Additional findings highlighted the importance of intergenerational knowledge transfer, representation, and safety in digital spaces, as well as the need for digital networks to function as containers of cultivation that pair online accessibility with embodied presence, creating healing spaces that honor both technological innovation and ancestral wisdom. This dissertation is available in open access at AURA (http://aura.antioch.edu) and the OhioLINK ETD Center (https://etd.ohiolink.edu/).
Keywords: Civil rights leaders; Civil Rights Movement; Contemplative action; Digital kinship; Digital spaces; Leadership

Title: A Case Study Of Millennial Nurses' Experiences Collaborating With Baby Boomer Personnel In A Medical Treatment Setting
Author: Fleming, Berlisher M.
Abstract: Millennial nurses are quickly becoming the largest generation in the nursing field, next to the baby boomers. As boomers retire, millennials are predicted to become the majority within the next 5 years. There is a lack of attention on the millennials’ experiences, skills transfer, policies to protect them, and training to prevent negative stereotyping from boomers. The purpose of this study was to gain a better understanding of millennial nurses’ experiences as they collaborate with baby boomer nurses in medical treatment settings. A descriptive qualitative case study methodology was used. There are numerous studies on baby boomers’ experiences as they lead or work with millennials. Still, there are very few studies from the millennials’ perspectives, and only a few on the generational workplace in the nursing field. Eight female participants were selected for the study—four Black, two Hispanic, and two White participants from seven states in the United States. Six themes emerged from the data that described the millennial nurses’ experiences of collaborating with baby boomers: boomers’ resistance to change, reverse ageism toward millennials, minimal knowledge transfer, boomers’ lack of technical skills, boomers’ uncollaborative behavior, and millennials' seeking alternative career opportunities. The study data demonstrated that millennials are eager to collaborate and learn from boomer nurses, despite the perception that the older, more experienced nurses are unwilling to teach them, and millennials desire to be respected for the skills they bring to the field, namely, technology. Collaboration and understanding can be accomplished between the two groups through role-playing in group training, generational and reverse mentoring, and new policies to protect millennial nurses, ensure skills transfer, and improve patient care. The findings of the study could help industrial/organization professionals develop training and policies to increase job satisfaction across generations and enhance retention and efficiency in the medical industry.
Keywords: Generational workplace; Knowledge transfer; Millennial nurses; Retention; Reverse ageism; Workplace collaboration

Title: Adaptive Machine Learning Frameworks Integrating Llms And Knowledge Graphs For Tailored Education
Author: Wasnik, Harsh Homdeo1; Shrivastava, Tushar11 University of Arizona,Tucson,Arizona,USA
Abstract: Conference Title: 2025 8th International Conference on Circuit, Power & Computing Technologies (ICCPCT) Conference Start Date: 2025 Aug. 7 Conference End Date: 2025 Aug. 8 Conference Location: Kollam, India Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have paved the way for innovative personalized education through Interactive Machine Learning Systems (IMLS). This study explores how combining large language models (LLMs) with knowledge graphs (KGs) within IMLS can create adaptive, customized learning experiences. Leveraging the sophisticated natural language understanding and generation capabilities of LLMs, the system enables dynamic interactions between learners and educational content, offering real-time feedback and personalized guidance. At the same time, knowledge graphs provide a well-organized framework for structuring educational materials, detailing the relationships among concepts, skills, and learner profiles. By integrating these technologies, the system can intelligently assess individual learner needs, predict learning trajectories, and tailor instructional strategies accordingly. Empirical evaluations across various educational settings have shown significant enhancements in learner engagement, knowledge retention, and overall academic performance. This research highlights the promising potential of merging advanced language models with semantic knowledge representations to develop scalable and effective personalized education solutions that cater to the unique needs of every learner. This study addresses the existing gap in combining large language models and knowledge graphs for personalized education by proposing a novel integrated framework. Our empirical results demonstrate significant improvements in learner engagement and knowledge retention, highlighting the robustness and scalability of the approach.
Keywords: Adaptive learning; Large language models; Scalability; Semantics; Knowledge graphs; Robustness; Real-time systems; Natural language processing; Trajectory; Standards; Personalized education; Interactive machine learning systems; Large language models; Knowledge graphs; Adaptive learning; Semantic knowledge representation

Title: Training Development And Learning Facilitation For Lower-Level Automated Driving Systems Across Age Groups
Author: Zheng, Haolan
Abstract: The adoption of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and other Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) introduces benefits and challenges for drivers. Drivers who use those driving technologies should develop accurate mental models, appropriately calibrate trust, and effectively apply automation in real-world scenarios. This dissertation examines how different text-based training influence drivers’ learning, engagement, and training transfer when using ACC, with a focus on both younger and older adults. A series of driving simulator studies compared several training conditions with or without different types of driving roles and responsibilities information. The study assessed how these training methods impact drivers’ comprehension, trust, and performance in edge-case driving scenarios. Results suggest that training presentation and structure significantly influences learning and transfer outcomes. Older participants, in particular, exhibited increased attention to system limitations, and scaffolding training content improved knowledge retention and application. These findings highlight the importance of structuring driver education programs to support better mental model development and trust calibration, ultimately enhancing the safe and effective use of ACC.
Keywords: Adaptive Cruise Control; Automation training; Driver responsibility; Scaffolding; Younger and older drivers

Title: From Creation To Diffusion To Application: The Evolution Of Ai Innovations From A Dynamic Network Perspective
Author: Gao, Kaige
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping global economies, yet we lack a holistic understanding of how AI innovations evolve across interconnected communities. This dissertation examines how interactions among heterogeneous actors and artifacts across open-source communities, academic fields, and commercial industries influence AI innovation's creation, diffusion, and application. Unlike previous research focusing on isolated breakthroughs, this study provides a comprehensive view of AI's evolutionary trajectory through a dynamic network perspective. The first introduces "network variability" as a novel construct to analyze how open-source collaboration networks adapt to changing environments, revealing that meaningful structural reconfigurations significantly enhance project development outcomes under increasing complexity. The second study examines how open-source AI models function as boundary objects bridging developer and academic communities, demonstrating that model network positioning influences scholarly impact through complex temporal patterns. The third study investigates cross-boundary knowledge transfer to industry contexts, analyzing how AI knowledge integration strategies affect patent generation and firm valuation in autonomous vehicles, revealing that exploration-oriented approaches yield greater market value than exploitation strategies. These findings establish AI innovation as a networked phenomenon with distinctive evolutionary dynamics that differ from traditional technologies, characterized by complex feedback loops across multiple interacting communities rather than a linear progression.
Keywords: Evolution of innovations; Dynamic network perspective; Open-source community; Innovation diffusion; Requisite variety theory

Title: Workforce Management Strategies For A Multigenerational Workforce: Challenges And Difficulties Of An Aging Workforce
Author: Bartels, Michael Edward
Abstract: The problem addressed in the study was that many organizations struggled with workforce generational differences as four generations coexisted in the labor force and Baby Boomers were leaving the workforce at an estimated four million a year leaving an ill-prepared workforce to handle the loss of institutional knowledge thus necessitating the need for multigenerational workforce management strategies. As a result, managers, supervisors, and organizations face challenges in continuing business as usual and merging diverse generational perspectives. The purpose of this qualitative single case study was to examine workforce generational differences, challenges posed by Baby Boomers leaving the workforce, and effective multigenerational workforce management strategies to address institutional knowledge loss. The study followed the Generational Cohort Theory, which helped explain how generational experiences play a role in shaping what happens in the workplace. This qualitative single-case study employed semi-structured interviews and demographic questionnaires with 20 participants aged 18 to 69 years from a traffic management company in northeast Oklahoma. Participants represented Baby Boomers, Generation X, Generation Y, and Generation Z cohorts. The analysis of data followed thematic coding guided by NVivo and Braun and Clarke’s step-by-step approach. Trustworthiness was established through triangulation, member checking, peer debriefing, and reflexive journaling. The results indicated that individuals from different generations communicated differently, used technology differently, and had differing work values and learning approaches. Baby Boomers preferred face-to-face communication, whereas digital communication was preferred by the younger generations. Key challenges included knowledge transfer barriers, differences in communication, and motivational factors. Successful management practices involved open communication structures, strength-based management approaches, mixed-generation teams, adaptive training methods, and formal mentoring programs. The study concludes that organizations must develop comprehensive, intentional management approaches, viewing generational diversity as a strategic advantage rather than a challenge. Among the recommendations included setting up knowledge-sharing programs, nurturing intelligence across ages among top managers, and encouraging environments that allow different age groups to contribute their ideas, leading to improved performance and creativity in organizations.
Keywords: Baby Boomers; Generation X; Generation Y; Generation Z; Multigenerational; Workforce

Title: Designing Everyday Family Technologies: Supporting Health And Wellbeing In Family-Food Interactions With Children
Author: Wu, Yuxing
Abstract: Technology plays an integral role in the fabric of everyday family life, supporting communication, child development, health, entertainment, and learning. Yet, designing interactive systems that are both meaningful and widely adopted (and genuinely embraced) by families remains a complex and evolving challenge. In fields like Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), there is a growing recognition of the need for more holistic and contextually grounded understandings of family life—ones that reflect the complexity of group interaction and diversity of family dynamics and domestic environments. My research seeks to address this gap through a family-centered lens, exploring how domestic technologies can be more thoughtfully designed to support the everyday social, health-related, and educational goals families encounter. My dissertation examines the context of family-food interactions as a key factor for understanding and designing everyday family technologies. First, I explore family meal practices through interviews and participatory design studies, uncovering the coordination and challenges involved in shared food routines and informing design implications. Second, I examine the lived experience of managing childhood food allergies, highlighting how families balance informational, emotional, and social needs while navigating evolving caregiving roles. Together, these studies shed light on how to design everyday family technologies that are attuned to the transitional, dynamic, and collaborative nature of family life. My dissertation contributes to the HCI and CSCW community by offering a multidimensional understanding of how families engage in food- and health-related practices and how we might better design for the complex realities of everyday family life. These contributions fall into three major areas [1]: (1) Empirical insights into holistic everyday family meal experiences alongside design implications for shared domestic technologies. Chapters 3 and 4 demonstrate that family meal processes are deeply embedded with emotional, symbolic, and relational meaning. They are more than routines—they are rituals, sites of tension, and evolving reflections of family life. My findings reveal how families maintain and adapt meal practices amid negotiation, care labor, and disagreements, highlighting that technology should not simply aim to eliminate conflict or optimize routines. Rather, systems should be designed to scaffold reflection, shared meaning-making, and the emotional work that underpins these everyday rituals. These insights point to a need for design approaches that value the expressive and relational dimensions of family life, not just coordination or efficiency.  (2) Theory-informed analysis of everyday family-food interactions. In Chapters 3, 4, and 5, I developed a theory-informed analysis that incorporates six primary conceptual framings: Family Routines and Rituals, Symbolic Interactionism Theory, Social Cognitive Theory, Family Systems Theory, Ecological Systems Theory, and Family Development Theory. These theoretical lenses provided the scaffolding to examine the subtleties of my empirical data and allowed for a deeper understanding of the developmental, affective, and relational dynamics at play in daily food routines and chronic care contexts. Rather than treating theory as background, I actively used these lenses to interpret how families distribute and negotiate responsibilities, manage change, and express care through food. During this process, I also proposed two sensitizing concepts, generative and systemic discontents (chapter 4), as conceptual framings that help characterize and categorize the social and emotional complexity of everyday family lives, as well as in embracing and designing for the messy reality of family interactions that are not always unanimous and celebratory.  (3) Reflections on the design of future family-centered systems that aim to provide sustainable, meaningful support for the evolving needs of diverse families. Drawing from all three studies, Chapter 6 articulates a forward-looking design agenda grounded in the lived messiness of family life. I argue that personal informatics should be reimagined as relational and ecological, with systems that support co-tracking, co-regulation, and intergenerational knowledge transfer. I advocate for designs that support developmental transitions—such as shifting care from parent to child—and that make visible the “invisible work” (often gendered and emotional) of family caregiving. I also propose technologies that can sustain and adapt with families over time, including AI-supported milestone tracking, collaborative dashboards, and ritual-aware notification systems.  In addition to design insights, I offer methodological contributions for studying and designing with families as a whole, not just for them. I demonstrate how family-based group interviews and remote participatory design sessions can serve as effective strategies for engaging the entire family as a unit of analysis. These methods allowed me to surface intra-family dynamics, multi-vocal perspectives, and co-constructed meanings that are often overlooked in individual interviews. By centering the family as a collective—rather than a sum of individuals—I was able to explore how responsibilities are negotiated, how care is distributed, and how technologies might better reflect the relational realities of everyday family life. Importantly, I also offer a gentle caution against over-reliance on AI and automation: everyday family care is inherently emotional, contextual, and negotiated, and technologies must be designed to enhance—not erase—these human dimensions.  Through these contributions, my dissertation provides both practical and theoretically informed insights and visions for designing interactive systems that are inclusive, developmentally sensitive, and attuned to the everyday complexity of family care.
Keywords: Family life; Food routines; Health informatics; Human-Computer Interaction; Interaction design and children; Computer-Supported Cooperative Work

Title: Interactive Cultural Narratives: Unveiling The Potential Of The 3D Serious Game Maker Tool For Kristang Culture
Author: Abdulrazic, Mostafa Osama Mostafa
Abstract: Culture is a cornerstone of identity, yet many cultures face the risk of fading into obscurity despite efforts to preserve them through various mediums. Leveraging the concept of 3D serious games and basing the research on key learning theories like the maker pedagogy and multiliteracies theory, this research aims to educate children about culture by developing a 3D game maker tool that implements key elements of the researched learning theories. The goal is to empower users aged 12 and older to effortlessly create serious 3D cultural games in a first-person view, enriching the experience with IoT and AI features. Additionally, the tool facilitates gameplay of games crafted using the same platform, fostering social interaction and aligning with effective forms of learning. Methodologically, an iterative design process was employed, starting with the creation of initial prototypes and interviews to discern optimal features and design principles. The tool was refined based on insights gathered from existing literature on serious game design guidelines, prioritizing user-friendly accessibility, and ensuring the learning theories are properly implemented. In the final trial, participants engaged in crafting 3D serious games using the tool, juxtaposed against a control group utilizing a conventional 2D storyboarding tool. Subsequently, participants immersed themselves in a serious game experience exploring Kristang culture, with learning gain, memory, and knowledge retention meticulously evaluated. Despite its focus on Kristang culture, the tool's adaptable nature suggests its potential applicability to diverse cultural contexts. Findings indicate that the tool improved user experience and learning outcomes for Kristang cultural heritage education compared to control methods. However, limitations such as a small sample size and system constraints were encountered. Research implications delve into the potential of the 3D serious game maker tool as an effective educational tool for children to learn about culture, contributing to the broader understanding of utilizing innovative technology for cultural education among younger demographics. Moreover, design guidelines established in the study lay a foundation for future research endeavors seeking to develop effective educational tools with a significant impact on learning outcomes. Practically, integrating the tool into classroom settings holds promise for fostering immersive and interactive cultural education experiences, augmenting traditional instruction, and facilitating collaborative learning. This research introduces an innovative educational tool that combines elements of the maker movement with experiential learning paradigms, addressing the gap of lack in 3D cultural maker educational tools that combine learning theories into their framework. This research aims to implement those learning theories into an engaging 3D game maker tool specifically for the Kristang culture. In conclusion, the development of this innovative 3D serious game maker tool represents a significant stride towards cultural education for younger generations. Embracing the opportunities presented by this tool can pave the way for more inclusive and dynamic approaches to cultural learning, empowering children to explore, create, and celebrate diverse cultural narratives in a digital age.

Title: Creating A Digital House Of Knowledge: Ensuring The Continuation And Integrity Of Indigenous Knowledge Transfers In The Digital Era
Author: Cordova, Jacquelyn C.
Abstract: Traditional Indigenous knowledge transfer systems have contributed to the sustainability of highly advanced Indigenous societies since time immemorial. These systems have been purposefully targeted since European arrival to the Americas in the late 1400s and early 1500s, consequently creating barriers for Indigenous Peoples as they strive to conduct their traditional knowledge transfer processes within their communities. These barriers, paired with the continual rise and use of global technologies, have created circumstances where many Native Elders and Knowledge Keepers experience difficulty reaching their youth and community members to ensure the successful transfer of Indigenous data and knowledge to current and future generations. This research applies Critical Indigenous Research Methodologies (CIRM), Participant Action Research (PAR), and Grounded Theory (GT), along with data generated from twenty-five project participants, to create a model of an Indigenous-made “Digital House of Knowledge” for those who seek to ensure the continuation and integrity of their distinct Indigenous knowledge transfer systems now and into the future. By discussing critical topics such as the security, privacy, and protection of data, processes for gathering, storing, sharing, accessing, and maintaining data, the design and structural components of digital platforms, and the importance and urgency of the application of traditional Indigenous protocols within the digital realm, this research provides a comprehensive overview of critical key points for the successful gathering, storage, protection, and dissemination of Indigenous data and knowledge within the digital realm.
Keywords: Application of traditional Indigenous protocols; Indigenous data management; Indigenous digital educational platforms; Indigenous knowledge; Indigenous sovereignty; Western and global technologies

Title: Organizational Learning Support Preferences Of Millennials: An Interpretive Study
Author: Thompson, Kevin S.11 University of Connecticut, US
Abstract: Given that more than $170 Billion is invested annually on employee learning and development in the U.S (American Society for Training and Development, 2011) it is important that training leads to improved job performance. In any organization, workers require new knowledge and skills to become productive and maintain performance over time. Millennials. who comprise the latest employee generation, are age 18-34 (Tyler, 2007) and share the same performance requirements and expectations of the generations that precede them in the workforce. Adult education and human resources development literature indicates that millennials prefer classroom, online, and blended learning experiences (Sandeen, 2008), and respond well to feedback (Gigante, Dell, & Sharkey, 2011) and learning technologies (Gavota, Cattaneo, Arn, Boldrini, Motta, Schneider, & Betrancourt, 2010) when used to support learning. In addition, there are many theoretical articles that propose what organizations should do to support learning for the millennial generation (Kirkland and Sheehan, 2010). b The organizational learning supports to help millennials improve their learning in the workplace remain largely undefined. For the purposes of this research effort, organizational learning supports are the tools and resources provided by an organization to promote knowledge transfer. To efficiently and effectively meet the learning goals set forth for millennials in workplace settings, scholars must research, and learning practitioners must consider the organizational learning supports that millennials prefer for learning. b To address this challenge, I explored the organizational learning support preferences of millennials in the workforce in a for-profit, high-technology corporation. High-technology role complexity requires significant learning for employees which created a fertile research environment. To establish a viable foundation for my research in a corporate environment, 1 identified a conceptual framework that incorporates the learner, learning experiences, and environmental (organizational) factors. One research question guided this study: What are millennials organizational learning support preferences? The study utilized an interpretative qualitative design (Merriam, 2002), that uncovered the experiences millennials had with organizational learning supports to understand which supports they prefer. The sample of ten millennial participants was purposeful and generated from a list of 100 employees participating in a corporation's leadership development program. Data was collected via two semi-structured interviews with eight participants and one semi-structured interview with two participants. Data was analyzed inductively using a constant-comparative method and yielded three themes: (a) millennials appreciate big-picture understanding, new information, and rapid application to help them learn and perform on the job, (b) millennials prefer having the option to learn independently or in small groups to deepen their understanding of new knowledge, and (c) millennials want resources that provide answers to questions that fill knowledge gaps. By gathering participant data regarding millennials' organizational learning support preferences, the body of adult education and human resources development literature can expand and learning practitioners in organizations can propose and offer learning supports that align with millennials preferences to improve learning for this latest generation of workplace employees. Keywords: Organizational learning support preferences; millennials; workforce; corporate environment; knowledge transfer; workplace settings

Title: Adressing Domain-Specific Knowledge And Terminology Using Large Language Models And Word Embeddings
Author: Saado, Hassan
Abstract: Knowledge-intensive domains, such as telecommunications, rely heavily on domain-specific terminology, which might create barriers to effective communication and knowledge transfer. This thesis investigates the application of Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, specifically Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Word2Vec models, to enhance knowledge retrieval and understanding of domain-specific terminology within the context of Ericsson's Cloud Radio Access Network (RAN) technology. The RAG approach modifies a general-purpose Large Language Model (LLM) to answer domain-specific questions by retrieving relevant information from a knowledge base. The best-performing RAG-based chatbot was the one that retrieved a single document, achieving a score of 207 out of a maximum of 300. Additionally, the RAG-based chatbot outperformed the general-purpose ChatGPT 3.5 model, which scored 144. Moreover, a Word2Vec model was trained on internal documentation to generate similar terms related to Cloud RAN terminology. The Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) architecture with an embedding size of 300, a window size of 5, and a cosine threshold of 0.9 achieved the best performance, with a precision of 0.71 and a recall of 0.70 in generating relevant similar terms. The thesis also presents an integrated solution combining the RAG-based chatbot and the Word2Vec model with an user interface. The RAG module generates accurate responses based on user queries and retrieved-context, while the Word2Vec module suggests related technical topics, facilitating further exploration and understanding.

Title: The Immersion Of The Technological And Psychological Aspects To Achieve The Usefulness And Successful Use Of An Information Systems
Author: Salvá Acevedo, Josue E.
Abstract: This research's purpose is to identify and measure the variables that have an effect towards the actual system usage in the workplace. A questionnaire-based survey was distributed through Microsoft Forms, and it retrieved 177 samples from working people whose daily tasks involved using information systems in their workplace. The participants' answers were used to analyze the effect technological attributes have on the perceived usefulness and how psychological aspects have an effect on the perceived ease of use; also, how both perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use positively affect the immersion of the employee. Additionally, the study analyzes the effect of immersion on actual system use. A multivariate analysis was utilized to acquire the measurement. A descriptive analysis was also used to identify the generational cohort, gender, and years of experience of the participants. The results indicate that when the employees immerse themselves in the information systems in the workplace, it has a positive effect that will cause them to use the actual systems. Such immersion is possible through various means, including knowledge transfer between employees and immersive learning by training the employees with the information systems inside the workplace.
Keywords: Psychological aspects; Employees; Information systems; Workplace; Technological attributes
